assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
The intragroup dividend has been declared and gives rise to a recognised intragroup receivable/payable, ie a monetary item.
The entities involved may have different functional currencies; otherwise the residual foreign exchange exposure test may not be met.
No facts indicate that the reporting entity is an investment entity or that the exposure is being analysed under that specific consolidation exception.
No facts indicate that the dividend exposure is being designated as part of a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: Intragroup monetary item hedge in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: An intragroup monetary item may qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if its foreign currency gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The dividend declaration creates a recognised intragroup monetary receivable/payable
The item is between group entities with different functional currencies
The foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge relationship is formally designated, documented and meets hedge effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: The FX risk on the dividend receivable/payable can be designated in consolidation only if a residual consolidated FX exposure remains.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 6.3.6 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when IAS 21 FX effects are not fully eliminated.
IFRS 9 6.4.1 requires designation, documentation and effectiveness testing for any qualifying hedge relationship.

approaches.label: General prohibition for intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: Hedged items must generally involve parties external to the reporting entity; intragroup items are not eligible in consolidated financial statements unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Absent the specific intragroup FX exception, the dividend exposure cannot be designated as a hedged item in consolidated accounts.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 6.3.5 restricts hedged items in consolidation to exposures with external parties, subject to narrow exceptions.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge
approaches.reasoning: A forecast intragroup transaction qualifies only if it is highly probable, foreign-currency denominated and its FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss; the context does not support that for dividends.
conditions: It is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the transacting entity
Its foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss
approaches.practical_implication: If the dividend is only forecast rather than recognised, hedge accounting is unlikely on the facts provided because a profit-or-loss effect is not evidenced.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9 6.3.6 allows highly probable forecast intragroup transactions as hedged items only when the FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.
IFRS 9 B6.3.5 explains that many intragroup items do not qualify unless there is a related external effect on consolidated profit or loss.
IFRS 9 6.3.3 requires a forecast transaction designated as a hedged item to be highly probable.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge alternative
approaches.reasoning: A net investment hedge applies to foreign currency risk on net assets of a foreign operation, not to an intragroup dividend receivable as such.
conditions: The hedged item is an amount of net assets of a foreign operation included in consolidated financial statements
The hedged risk is foreign currency risk between the foreign operation and a parent entity functional currency
The relationship satisfies IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: If the objective is to hedge FX risk on foreign operations, the structure must be a net investment hedge rather than a hedge of the dividend receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: IFRIC 16 states that the hedged item in a net investment hedge is an amount of net assets of the foreign operation included in the consolidated financial statements.
IFRIC 16 paragraph 14 requires IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness, while allowing the hedging instrument to be held anywhere in the group.
IFRS 9 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge accounting model.
